title: Spinning a DK-weight yarn on a drop spindle
subtitle: Drafting and plying on a top-whorl spindle to produce a consistent DK
excerpt: DK weight is the most versatile handspun yarn weight for both knitting and weaving. This entry covers setting up a top-whorl spindle, maintaining a consistent draft for DK singles, and plying two bobbins into a balanced two-ply using the spindle alone.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from standard drop-spindle spinning practice. DK-weight spindle spinning is the standard first project taught in British spinning guilds.

glossaryTerms.definition: The weighted disc at the top (top-whorl) or bottom (bottom-whorl) of a drop spindle that stores the spin momentum and determines the ratio of twist to draft.
The first continuous yarn produced directly from the fibre supply on the spindle. Singles carry active twist and must be plied in the opposite direction to produce a balanced yarn.

A spindle technique in which the spindle is hooked or parked on the knee while the hands draft the fibre, then released to continue spinning. Recommended for beginners as it separates the drafting and spinning actions.
Wraps per inch: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler in one inch. DK-weight yarn runs at around 11 to 14 wraps per inch in the singles.

content.text: A top-whorl drop spindle with a
diameter of about 7 cm and a weight of 40 to 50 g is the right spindle for DK-weight spinning. Heavier spindles produce thicker yarn; lighter spindles draw the yarn finer than DK. The
method suits beginners because it lets you focus on one action at a time. Use a commercially combed top of Corriedale or Romney for the first project; both breeds draft predictably at this weight.

items.name: Combed top, 4-10 cm staple (Corriedale or Romney)

content.text: Tie a leader yarn about 30 cm long to the shaft, wind it up toward the whorl several times, and loop it over the hook at the top. Pre-draft about 1 m of top by pulling the bundle to about double its original width; this opens the fibres ready for easy drafting. Attach the pre-drafted fibre to the leader by overlapping the ends.

Give the spindle a clockwise spin with the thumb and forefinger. Let it drop and begin to spin while you hold the fibre supply in your upper hand. Use park-and-draft until you feel comfortable: park the spindle by resting it on your knee, draft back with both hands to the desired length, release the spindle, and allow the twist to run into the drafted zone. Wind the spun yarn onto the shaft, leaving a small amount below the whorl, and repeat.
: wrap the yarn around a ruler and count how many wraps fit in one inch. Aim for 22 to 28 wraps per inch in the singles; this will produce a DK-weight finished two-ply at 11 to 14 wraps per inch.

Wind the full cop of singles off onto a niddy-noddy to make a hank, then wind it into a ball. Use the Andean bracelet method or a centre-pull ball to feed two ends from the same hank back to the spindle and ply counterclockwise (opposite to the spin direction). Wind off onto the niddy-noddy again, tie the hank in four places, soak in lukewarm water for 20 minutes, press out water in a towel, and hang to dry with a small weight to set the twist.

items.problem: The spindle spins back and untwists while drafting
items.cause: Not enough twist was added before drafting, or the spindle is too heavy for the fibre weight
items.fix: Give the spindle more vigorous initial spin before drafting. A more energetic spin stores more momentum. If the spindle is over 60 g, try a lighter model for finer work.

items.cause: Too little twist in the drafting zone, or drafting beyond the staple length of the fibre
items.fix: Allow more twist into the drafting zone before drawing back. Keep the back hand within 10 cm of the front hand to avoid drafting past the staple length.
